Abaeis.description: Eurema is a widespread genus of grass yellow butterflies in the family Pieridae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Stijn De Win, all rights reserved, uploaded by Stijn De Win

Abies.description: Firs are evergreen coniferous trees belonging to the genus Abies in the family Pinaceae. There are approximately 48–65 extant species, found on mountains throughout much of North and Central America, Eurasia, and North Africa. The genus is most closely related to Keteleeria, a small genus confined to eastern Asia.
Abies.funFact: The genus is most closely related to Keteleeria, a small genus confined to eastern Asia.
Abies.nativeRegion: There are approximately 48–65 extant species, found on mountains throughout much of North and Central America, Eurasia, and North Africa.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) emilio2020, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by emilio2020

Abisara.description: Abisara is a genus of butterflies in the family Riodinidae, with species found only in Asia. The genus formerly included African species but it was found to be polyphyletic in 2015. Several species formerly included in the genus were then moved to Afriodinia, Archigenes, Sibosia and Spitosa.
Abisara.funFact: Abisara is a genus of butterflies in the family Riodinidae, with species found only in Asia.
Abisara.nativeRegion: Abisara is a genus of butterflies in the family Riodinidae, with species found only in Asia.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) chathura_udayanga, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

Abortiporus.description: Abortiporus is a genus of fungi in the family Meruliaceae. The widely distributed genus contains three species. Species in the genus grow on the wood of hardwoods and conifers, either alone or around the stumps and living trees. It causes a white rot in dead wood and a white trunk rot in living wood. The genus was circumscribed in 1904 by William Alphonso Murrill. The generic name is derived from the Latin abortus and the Ancient Greek πόρος (pore).
Abortiporus.funFact: Species in the genus grow on the wood of hardwoods and conifers, either alone or around the stumps and living trees.
Abortiporus.nativeRegion: The widely distributed genus contains three species.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Alan Rockefeller, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Alan Rockefeller

Abracris.description: Abracris is a genus of short-horned grasshoppers in the family Acrididae. There are at least three described species in Abracris, found in North, Central, and South America.
Abracris.nativeRegion: There are at least three described species in Abracris, found in North, Central, and South America.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Juan Manuel Cardona Granda, all rights reserved

Abraxas.description: Abraxas, the magpie moths, is a genus of moths in the family Geometridae. It was first described by William Elford Leach in 1815.
Abraxas.funFact: It was first described by William Elford Leach in 1815.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Jatishwor Irungbam, all rights reserved, uploaded by Jatishwor Irungbam

Abronia.description: Abronia, the sand-verbenas or wild lantanas, is a genus of about 20 species of annual or perennial herbaceous plants in the family Nyctaginaceae. Despite the common names, they are not related to Verbena (vervains) or lantanas in the family Verbenaceae. They are closely allied with Tripterocalyx.
Abronia.funFact: Despite the common names, they are not related to Verbena (vervains) or lantanas in the family Verbenaceae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by Al Kordesch

Abrus.description: Abrus is a genus of flowering plants in the pea family, Fabaceae, and the only genus in the tribe Abreae. It contains 13–18 species, but is best known for a single species: jequirity. The highly toxic seeds of that species are used to make jewellery.
Abrus.funFact: Abrus is a genus of flowering plants in the pea family, Fabaceae, and the only genus in the tribe Abreae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Dinesh Valke, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

Abudefduf.description: Abudefduf is a genus of fish in the family Pomacentridae. They are also known as the sergeant-majors The name Abudefduf comes from Arabic word ابو دفدوف abu, "the one with"; and def, "side", and the intensive plural ending -duf. Thus, the genus' name means "the one with prominent sides".
Abudefduf.funFact: They are also known as the sergeant-majors The name Abudefduf comes from Arabic word ابو دفدوف abu, "the one with"; and def, "side", and the intensive plural ending -duf.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) JeffreyGammon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by JeffreyGammon

Abutilon.description:
Abutilon is a large genus of flowering plants in the mallow family, Malvaceae. It is distributed throughout the tropics and subtropics of the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Australia. General common names include Indian mallow and velvetleaf; ornamental varieties may be known as room maple, parlor maple, or flowering maple.
The genus name is an 18th-century Neo-Latin word that came from the Arabic ’abū-ṭīlūn, the name given by Avicenna to this or a similar genus.
Abutilon.funFact: General common names include Indian mallow and velvetleaf; ornamental varieties may be known as room maple, parlor maple, or flowering maple.
Abutilon.nativeRegion: It is distributed throughout the tropics and subtropics of the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Australia.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Subhajit Roy, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND), uploaded by Subhajit Roy

Acacia.description: Acacia, commonly known as wattles or acacias, is a genus of about 1,084 species of shrubs and trees in the subfamily Mimosoideae of the pea family Fabaceae. Initially, it comprised a group of plant species native to Africa, South America, and Australasia, but is now reserved for species mainly from Australia, with others from New Guinea, Southeast Asia, and the Indian Ocean.
Acacia.funFact: Acacia, commonly known as wattles or acacias, is a genus of about 1,084 species of shrubs and trees in the subfamily Mimosoideae of the pea family Fabaceae.
Acacia.nativeRegion: Initially, it comprised a group of plant species native to Africa, South America, and Australasia, but is now reserved for species mainly from Australia, with others from New Guinea, Southeast Asia, and the Indian Ocean.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) mjcorreia, all rights reserved, uploaded by mjcorreia

Acaena.description: Acaena is a genus of about 60 species of mainly evergreen, creeping herbaceous perennial plants and subshrubs in the family Rosaceae, native mainly to the Southern Hemisphere, notably New Zealand, Australia and South America, but with a few species extending into the Northern Hemisphere, north to Hawaii and California.
Acaena.funFact: Acaena is a genus of about 60 species of mainly evergreen, creeping herbaceous perennial plants and subshrubs in the family Rosaceae, native mainly to the Southern Hemisphere, notably New Zealand, Australia and South America, but with a few species extending into the Northern Hemisphere, north to Hawaii and California.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Colin Jacobs, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA)

Acalymma.description: Acalymma is a genus of leaf beetles found mainly in the New World. Approximately 72 species have been described in the Western Hemisphere.
Acalymma.funFact: Approximately 72 species have been described in the Western Hemisphere.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Nick Block, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Nick Block

Acalypha.description: Acalypha is a genus of flowering plants in the family Euphorbiaceae. It is the sole genus of the subtribe Acalyphinae. It is one of the largest euphorb genera, with approximately 450 to 462 species. The genus name Acalypha is from the Ancient Greek ἀκαλύφη (akalúphē) ("nettle"), an alternative form of ἀκαλήφη (akalḗphē), and was inspired by the nettle-like leaves. General common names include copperleaf and three-seeded mercury. Native North American species are generally inconspicuous most of the year until the fall when their stems and foliage turn a distinctive coppery-red.
Acalypha.funFact: It is one of the largest euphorb genera, with approximately 450 to 462 species.

defaultPhoto.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by 葉子

Acanalonia.description: Acanalonia is a genus of planthopper and contains the majority of the species within the family Acanaloniidae. Species have been recorded from southern Europe and the Americas.
Acanalonia.funFact: Species have been recorded from southern Europe and the Americas.

Aceria.description: Aceria is a genus of mites belonging to the family Eriophyidae, the gall mites. These tiny animals are parasites of plants. Several species can cause blistering and galls, including erineum galls. A few are economically significant pests, while others are useful as agents of biological pest control of invasive plants such as rush skeletonweed, creeping thistle, and field bindweed. There are over 900 species in the genus.
Aceria.funFact: A few are economically significant pests, while others are useful as agents of biological pest control of invasive plants such as rush skeletonweed, creeping thistle, and field bindweed.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Matt, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Matt

Achaea.description: Achaea is a genus of moths in the family Erebidae described by Jacob Hübner in 1923.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) C_J_W, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by C_J_W

Achalarus.description: Cecropterus is a genus of skippers in the family Hesperiidae, within which it is placed in subtribe Eudamina. It has three subgenera: Cecropterus, Thorybes and Murgaria.
Achalarus.funFact: It has three subgenera: Cecropterus, Thorybes and Murgaria.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Francisco Farriols Sarabia, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Francisco Farriols Sarabia

Acharia.description: Acharia is a genus of moths in the family Limacodidae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Andy Reago & Chrissy McClarren, some rights reserved (CC BY)

Achatia.description: Achatia is a moth genus in the family Noctuidae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Andy Reago & Chrissy McClarren, some rights reserved (CC BY)

Acherontia.description: The name death's-head hawkmoth refers to any of three moth species of the genus Acherontia. The former species is found throughout Africa and in Europe, the latter two are Asian; most uses of the common name refer to the African species. These moths are easily distinguishable by the vaguely human skull-shaped pattern of markings on the thorax. They are large nocturnal moths with brown and yellow or orange coloring, and all three species are fairly similar in size, coloration and life cycle.
Acherontia.funFact: These moths are easily distinguishable by the vaguely human skull-shaped pattern of markings on the thorax.
Acherontia.nativeRegion: The former species is found throughout Africa and in Europe, the latter two are Asian; most uses of the common name refer to the African species.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Roger C. Kendrick, all rights reserved

Achillea.description: Achillea is a genus of flowering plants in the family Asteraceae. The plants typically have frilly leaves and are known colloquially as yarrows, although this common name usually refers to A. millefolium. The genus was named after the Greek mythological character Achilles, whose soldiers were said to have used yarrow to treat their wounds; this is reflected by common names such as allheal and bloodwort. The genus is native primarily to Eurasia and North America.
Achillea.funFact: The genus was named after the Greek mythological character Achilles, whose soldiers were said to have used yarrow to treat their wounds; this is reflected by common names such as allheal and bloodwort.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) gdeheij, all rights reserved

Achlyodes.description: Achlyodes is a genus of skippers. It is monotypic, being represented by the single species Achlyodes busirus.
Achlyodes.funFact: It is monotypic, being represented by the single species Achlyodes busirus.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Carlos Schmidtutz, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Carlos Schmidtutz

Achlys.description: Achlys is a small genus of flowering plants in the barberry family (Berberidaceae), which it shares with genera such as Berberis and Vancouveria. It is named after the Greek legendary figure associated with shade and mist, Achlys, because the plants grow in the shade.
Achlys.funFact: It is named after the Greek legendary figure associated with shade and mist, Achlys, because the plants grow in the shade.

Acris.description: Cricket frogs, genus Acris, are small, North American frogs of the family Hylidae. They occur in northern Mexico (Coahuila), the United States east of the Rocky Mountains, and in southern Ontario, Canada.
Acris.funFact: They occur in northern Mexico (Coahuila), the United States east of the Rocky Mountains, and in southern Ontario, Canada.

Actophilornis.description: Actophilornis is a genus of jacana. It contains two species restricted to Africa and its surrounding islands.
Actophilornis.funFact: It contains two species restricted to Africa and its surrounding islands.

Adelges.description: Adelges is a genus of insects which feed on conifers. They have complex life cycles, some species feeding exclusively on spruce, others feeding on spruce and an alternate conifer. However, galls characteristic of each species are formed only on spruce. Six generations are usually needed to complete the 2-year cycle, and in the case of species having an alternate host, winged adults about 2 mm long are formed only in the generations that move from one host to the other.
Adelges.funFact: However, galls characteristic of each species are formed only on spruce.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Matthias Buck, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Matthias Buck

Adelinia.description: Adelinia grandis, previously known as Cynoglossum grande, is a species of flowering plant commonly known as hound's tongue in the borage family. It is the only species in the genus Adelinia.
Adelinia.funFact: Adelinia grandis, previously known as Cynoglossum grande, is a species of flowering plant commonly known as hound's tongue in the borage family.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Raphaela E. Floreani Buzbee, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Raphaela E. Floreani Buzbee

Adelpha.description: Adelpha is a genus of brush-footed butterflies found from the southern United States and Mexico to South America. They are commonly known as sisters, due to the white markings on their wings, which resemble a nun's habit. This genus is sometimes included with the admiral butterflies (Limenitis).
Adelpha.funFact: They are commonly known as sisters, due to the white markings on their wings, which resemble a nun's habit.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Gregory Greene, all rights reserved, uploaded by Gregory Greene

Adelphocoris.description: Adelphocoris is a genus of capsid bugs in the tribe Mirini. It is part of a genus group that now includes Creontiades, Megacoelum and Pseudomegacoelum.
Adelphocoris.funFact: It is part of a genus group that now includes Creontiades, Megacoelum and Pseudomegacoelum.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Paul Cook, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Paul Cook

Adenocaulon.description: Adenocaulon is a small genus of flowering plants in the family daisy family known generally as trailplants. It was first described in 1829.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Tony Iwane, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Tony Iwane

Adenophyllum.description: Adenophyllum is a small genus of plants in the tribe Tageteae within the family Asteraceae. It contains ten species known generally as dogweeds.
Adenophyllum.funFact: It contains ten species known generally as dogweeds.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) María Eugenia Mendiola González, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-SA), uploaded by María Eugenia Mendiola González

Adenostoma.description: Adenostoma is a genus of shrubs in the rose family (Rosaceae) containing only two species, chamise and redshanks. Both are native to the Californias.
Adenostoma.funFact: Adenostoma is a genus of shrubs in the rose family (Rosaceae) containing only two species, chamise and redshanks.

Adiantum.description: Adiantum, the maidenhair fern, is a genus of about 250 species of ferns in the subfamily Vittarioideae of the family Pteridaceae, though some researchers place it in its own family, Adiantaceae. The genus name comes from Greek, meaning "unwetted", referring to the fronds' ability to shed water without becoming wet.
Adiantum.funFact: The genus name comes from Greek, meaning "unwetted", referring to the fronds' ability to shed water without becoming wet.

Adonis.description: Adonis is a genus of about 20–30 species of flowering plants in the buttercup family, Ranunculaceae, native to Europe and Asia.
Adonis.funFact: Adonis is a genus of about 20–30 species of flowering plants in the buttercup family, Ranunculaceae, native to Europe and Asia.

Aedes.description: Aedes is a genus of mosquitoes originally found in tropical and subtropical zones, but now found on all continents except Antarctica. Some species have been spread by human activity: Aedes albopictus, a particularly invasive species, was spread to the Americas, including the United States, in the 1980s, by the used-tire trade.
Aedes.funFact: Some species have been spread by human activity: Aedes albopictus, a particularly invasive species, was spread to the Americas, including the United States, in the 1980s, by the used-tire trade.

Agave.description: Agave is a genus of monocots native to the arid regions of the Americas. The genus is primarily known for its succulent and xerophytic species that typically form large rosettes of strong, fleshy leaves.
Agave.funFact: Agave is a genus of monocots native to the arid regions of the Americas.

Agelaius.description: Agelaius is a genus of blackbirds in the New World family Icteridae. Established by Louis Pierre Vieillot in 1816, it contains five species:

Agelastica.description: Agelastica is a genus of beetles in the family Chrysomelidae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) David Renoult, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by David Renoult

Ageratina.description: Ageratina, commonly known as snakeroot, is a genus of over 300 species of perennials and rounded shrubs in the family Asteraceae.
Ageratina.funFact: Ageratina, commonly known as snakeroot, is a genus of over 300 species of perennials and rounded shrubs in the family Asteraceae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Cora Cunningham, all rights reserved, uploaded by Cora Cunningham

Ageratum.description: Ageratum is a genus of 40 to 60 tropical and warm temperate flowering annuals and perennials from the family Asteraceae, tribe Eupatorieae. Most species are native to Central America and Mexico but four are native to the United States.
Ageratum.funFact: Most species are native to Central America and Mexico but four are native to the United States.
Ageratum.nativeRegion: Most species are native to Central America and Mexico but four are native to the United States.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) amantedarmanin, some rights reserved (CC BY)

Agkistrodon.description: Agkistrodon is a genus of pit vipers commonly known as American moccasins. The genus is endemic to North America, ranging from the northeastern United States to northern Costa Rica. Eight species are recognized as being valid, all of them monotypic and closely related. Common names include: cottonmouths, copperheads, and cantils.
Agkistrodon.funFact: Agkistrodon is a genus of pit vipers commonly known as American moccasins.
Agkistrodon.nativeRegion: The genus is endemic to North America, ranging from the northeastern United States to northern Costa Rica.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Kirk, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Kirk

Aglais.description: Aglais is a Holarctic genus of brush-footed butterflies, containing the tortoiseshells. This genus is sometimes indicated as a subgenus of Nymphalis or simply being an unnecessary division from the genus Nymphalis, which also includes tortoiseshells, but it is usually considered to be separate. This proposed separate genus is also considered "brushfooted butterflies" historically together with the other or separate Nymphalis species.
Aglais.funFact: This genus is sometimes indicated as a subgenus of Nymphalis or simply being an unnecessary division from the genus Nymphalis, which also includes tortoiseshells, but it is usually considered to be separate.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Subhajit Roy, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND), uploaded by Subhajit Roy

Aglossa.description: Aglossa is a genus of small moths belonging to the family Pyralidae. It was described by Pierre André Latreille in 1796. They are found mainly in western Eurasia, though some species have been introduced elsewhere.
Aglossa.funFact: They are found mainly in western Eurasia, though some species have been introduced elsewhere.

Agnorisma.description: Agnorisma is a genus of moths of the family Noctuidae. Agnorisma species were formerly included in the genus Xestia.
Agnorisma.funFact: Agnorisma species were formerly included in the genus Xestia.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Summit Metro Parks, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Summit Metro Parks

Agonopterix.description: Agonopterix is a moth genus of the superfamily Gelechioidea. It is placed in the family Depressariidae, which was often – particularly in older treatments – considered a subfamily of the Oecophoridae or included in the Elachistidae.
Agonopterix.funFact: It is placed in the family Depressariidae, which was often – particularly in older treatments – considered a subfamily of the Oecophoridae or included in the Elachistidae.

Agoseris.description: Agoseris is a small genus of annual or perennial herbs in the family Asteraceae described as a genus in 1817.

Agraulis.description: The Gulf fritillary or passion butterfly is a bright orange butterfly in the subfamily Heliconiinae of the family Nymphalidae. That subfamily was formerly set apart as a separate family, the Heliconiidae. The Heliconiinae are "longwing butterflies", which have long, narrow wings compared to other butterflies.
Agraulis.funFact: That subfamily was formerly set apart as a separate family, the Heliconiidae.

Agrilus.description: Agrilus is a genus of jewel beetles, notable for having the largest number of species of any single genus in the animal kingdom. Species of the genus have a cosmopolitan distribution on all continents except Antarctica.
Agrilus.funFact: Agrilus is a genus of jewel beetles, notable for having the largest number of species of any single genus in the animal kingdom.

Agrimonia.description: Agrimonia, commonly known as agrimony, is a genus of 12–15 species of perennial herbaceous flowering plants in the family Rosaceae, native to the temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with one species also in Africa. The species grow to between 0.5–2 m (1.6–6.6 ft) tall, with interrupted pinnate leaves, and tiny yellow flowers borne on a single spike.
Agrimonia.funFact: Agrimonia, commonly known as agrimony, is a genus of 12–15 species of perennial herbaceous flowering plants in the family Rosaceae, native to the temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with one species also in Africa.
Agrimonia.nativeRegion: Agrimonia, commonly known as agrimony, is a genus of 12–15 species of perennial herbaceous flowering plants in the family Rosaceae, native to the temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with one species also in Africa.

Agriocnemis.description:
Agriocnemis is a genus of damselfly in the family Coenagrionidae.
Agriocnemis is distributed widely across Africa, South-east Asia, Indonesia, Australia and islands in the Pacific.They are small insects, commonly known as wisps.
Agriocnemis.funFact: Agriocnemis is distributed widely across Africa, South-east Asia, Indonesia, Australia and islands in the Pacific.They are small insects, commonly known as wisps.
Agriocnemis.nativeRegion: Agriocnemis is distributed widely across Africa, South-east Asia, Indonesia, Australia and islands in the Pacific.They are small insects, commonly known as wisps.

Agropyron.description: Agropyron is a genus of Eurasian plants in the grass family), native to Europe and Asia but widely naturalized in North America.
Agropyron.funFact: Agropyron is a genus of Eurasian plants in the grass family), native to Europe and Asia but widely naturalized in North America.
Agropyron.nativeRegion: Agropyron is a genus of Eurasian plants in the grass family), native to Europe and Asia but widely naturalized in North America.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Donald Hobern, some rights reserved (CC BY)
(c) Sarefo, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)

Ajuga.description: Ajuga, also known as bugleweed, ground pine, carpet bugle, or just bugle, is a genus of flowering plants in the Ajugeae tribe of the mint family Lamiaceae. Over 60 species of annual or perennial, mostly herbaceous plants are known. They are native to Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia.
Ajuga.funFact: They are native to Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia.
Ajuga.nativeRegion: They are native to Europe, Asia, Africa, and Australia.

Alligator.description: An alligator, or colloquially gator, is a large reptile in the genus Alligator of the family Alligatoridae in the order Crocodilia. The two extant species are the American alligator and the Chinese alligator. Additionally, several extinct species of alligator are known from fossil remains. Alligators first appeared during the late Eocene epoch about 37 million years ago.
Alligator.funFact: Alligators first appeared during the late Eocene epoch about 37 million years ago.

Allium.description: Allium is a large genus of monocotyledonous flowering plants with 1112 accepted species, making Allium the largest genus in the family Amaryllidaceae and among the largest plant genera in the world. Many of the species are edible, and some have a long history of cultivation and human consumption such as the onion, garlic, scallions, shallots, leeks, and chives.
Allium.funFact: Allium is a large genus of monocotyledonous flowering plants with 1112 accepted species, making Allium the largest genus in the family Amaryllidaceae and among the largest plant genera in the world.

Alocasia.description: Alocasia is a genus of rhizomatous or tuberous, broad-leaved, perennial, flowering plants from the family Araceae. There are about 90 accepted species native to tropical and subtropical Asia and eastern Australia. Around the world, many growers widely cultivate a range of hybrids and cultivars as ornamentals.
Alocasia.funFact: There are about 90 accepted species native to tropical and subtropical Asia and eastern Australia.
Alocasia.nativeRegion: There are about 90 accepted species native to tropical and subtropical Asia and eastern Australia.

Aloe.description: Aloe is a genus containing over 650 species of flowering succulent plants. The most widely known species is Aloe vera, or "true aloe". It is called this because it is cultivated as the standard source for assorted pharmaceutical purposes. Other species, such as Aloe ferox, are also cultivated or harvested from the wild for similar applications.
Aloe.funFact: The most widely known species is Aloe vera, or "true aloe".

Alopecurus.description: Alopecurus, or foxtail grass, is a common and widespread genus of plants in the grass family. It is common across temperate and subtropical parts of Eurasia, northern Africa, and the Americas, as well as naturalized in Australia and on various islands.
Alopecurus.funFact: It is common across temperate and subtropical parts of Eurasia, northern Africa, and the Americas, as well as naturalized in Australia and on various islands.

Alophia.description: Alophia is a small genus of perennial, herbaceous and bulbous plants in the family Iridaceae. The genus comprise five known species that occur from the South-central United States as well as in Mexico, Central America, and parts of South America.
Alophia.funFact: The genus comprise five known species that occur from the South-central United States as well as in Mexico, Central America, and parts of South America.

Alopochen.description: Alopochen is a genus of the bird family Anatidae, part of the subfamily Tadorninae along with the shelducks. It contains one extant species, the Egyptian goose, and three which became extinct in the last 1,000 years or so. The Egyptian goose is native to mainland Africa, and the recently extinct species are from Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands.
Alopochen.funFact: The Egyptian goose is native to mainland Africa, and the recently extinct species are from Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands.
Alopochen.nativeRegion: The Egyptian goose is native to mainland Africa, and the recently extinct species are from Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) frank-h, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

Aloysia.description: Aloysia is a genus of flowering plants in the verbena family, Verbenaceae. They are known generally as beebrushes. They are native to the Americas, where they are distributed in temperate climates, as well as in subtropical and desert climates. The genus is named for Maria Luisa of Parma (1751-1819), wife of King Charles IV of Spain.
Aloysia.funFact: They are native to the Americas, where they are distributed in temperate climates, as well as in subtropical and desert climates.
Aloysia.nativeRegion: They are native to the Americas, where they are distributed in temperate climates, as well as in subtropical and desert climates.

Alpinia.description: Alpinia is a genus of flowering plants in the ginger family, Zingiberaceae. Species are native to Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, where they occur in tropical and subtropical climates. Several species are cultivated as ornamental plants.
Alpinia.funFact: Species are native to Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, where they occur in tropical and subtropical climates.
Alpinia.nativeRegion: Species are native to Asia, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, where they occur in tropical and subtropical climates.

Alseuosmia.description: Alseuosmia is a genus of five species of flowering plants in the family Alseuosmiaceae, growing in New Zealand's North Island and in the northern parts of the South Island. Species members are characteristically small evergreen shrubs. An example occurrence of species representative Alseuosmia macrophylla is in the habitat of the Hamilton Ecological District, where Blechnum discolor and B. filiforme are understory elements with a Nothofagus truncata and Dacrydium cupressinum overstory.
Alseuosmia.funFact: Species members are characteristically small evergreen shrubs.

Alternanthera.description: Alternanthera is a genus of flowering plants in the family Amaranthaceae. It is a widespread genus with most species occurring in the tropical Americas, and others in Asia, Africa, and Australia. Plants of the genus may be known generally as joyweeds, or Joseph's coat. Several species are notorious noxious weeds.
Alternanthera.funFact: It is a widespread genus with most species occurring in the tropical Americas, and others in Asia, Africa, and Australia.

defaultPhoto.attribution: no rights reserved, uploaded by 葉子

Alypia.description: Alypia is a genus of moths in the family Noctuidae.

Amaryllis.description: Amaryllis is the only genus in the subtribe Amaryllidinae. It is a small genus of flowering bulbs, with two species. The better known of the two, Amaryllis belladonna, is a native of the Western Cape region of South Africa, particularly the rocky southwest area between the Olifants River Valley and Knysna.
Amaryllis.funFact: Amaryllis is the only genus in the subtribe Amaryllidinae.

Ambrosia.description: In the ancient Greek myths, ambrosia is the food or drink of the Greek gods, and is often depicted as conferring longevity or immortality upon whoever consumed it. It was brought to the gods in Olympus by doves and served either by Hebe or by Ganymede at the heavenly feast.
Ambrosia.funFact: It was brought to the gods in Olympus by doves and served either by Hebe or by Ganymede at the heavenly feast.

Amphispiza.description: The black-throated sparrow is a species of bird in the family Passerellidae, the New World sparrows. It is found in Mexico and the United States.
Amphispiza.nativeRegion: It is found in Mexico and the United States.

Anabrus.description: Anabrus is a genus of insects in the family Tettigoniidae that includes the Mormon cricket.

Anacamptis.description: Anacamptis is a genus of flowering plants in the orchid family (Orchidaceae); it is often abbreviated as Ant in horticulture. This genus was established by Louis Claude Richard in 1817; the type species is the pyramidal orchid and it nowadays contains about one-third of the species placed in the "wastebin genus" Orchis before this was split up at the end of the 20th century, among them many that are of hybrid origin. The genus' scientific name is derived from the Greek word anakamptein, meaning "to bend backwards".
Anacamptis.funFact: This genus was established by Louis Claude Richard in 1817; the type species is the pyramidal orchid and it nowadays contains about one-third of the species placed in the "wastebin genus" Orchis before this was split up at the end of the 20th century, among them many that are of hybrid origin.

Anacardium.description: Anacardium, the cashews, are a genus of flowering plants in the family Anacardiaceae, native to tropical regions of the Americas. The best known species is Anacardium occidentale, which is commercially cultivated for its cashew nuts and cashew apples.
Anacardium.funFact: Anacardium, the cashews, are a genus of flowering plants in the family Anacardiaceae, native to tropical regions of the Americas.
Anacardium.nativeRegion: Anacardium, the cashews, are a genus of flowering plants in the family Anacardiaceae, native to tropical regions of the Americas.

Anacridium.description: Anacridium is a genus of "tree locusts" or "bird grasshoppers" belonging to the subfamily Cyrtacanthacridinae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Manuel Raab, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Manuel Raab

Anageshna.description: Anageshna is a monotypic moth genus of the family Crambidae described by Eugene G. Munroe in 1956. Its only species, Anageshna primordialis, the yellow-spotted webworm, was described by Harrison Gray Dyar Jr. in 1907. It is found in the US states of Alabama, Arkansas, Arizona, Florida, Georgia, Iowa, Illinois, Kansas, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Maryland, Maine, Minnesota, Missouri, Mississippi, North Carolina, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Ohio, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Texas, Virginia and West Virginia.
Anageshna.funFact: Its only species, Anageshna primordialis, the yellow-spotted webworm, was described by Harrison Gray Dyar Jr.
Anageshna.nativeRegion: It is found in the US states of Alabama, Arkansas, Arizona, Florida, Georgia, Iowa, Illinois, Kansas, Louisiana, Massachusetts, Maryland, Maine, Minnesota, Missouri, Mississippi, North Carolina, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New York, Ohio, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Texas, Virginia and West Virginia.

Anagrapha.description: Anagrapha is a genus of moths of the family Noctuidae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Anita, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

Analeptura.description:
Analeptura is a genus of beetles in the family Cerambycidae, containing the following species:
Analeptura fallax (Say, 1824)
Analeptura lineola (Say, 1824)

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) David H. Cox, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by David H. Cox
(c) Stefan, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Stefan

Anaphalioides.description:
Anaphalioides is a flowering plant genus in the Asteraceae family, described as a genus in 1950. They are native to New Zealand and New Guinea.SpeciesAnaphalioides alpina (Cockayne) D.Glenny - New Zealand
Anaphalioides bellidioides (G.Forst.) D.Glenny - New Zealand incl. Antipodean + Chatham Islands
Anaphalioides hookeri (Allan) Anderb. - New Zealand South Island
Anaphalioides mariae (F.Muell.) Glenny - New Zealand, Papua New Guinea
Anaphalioides papuana (F.Muell.) Glenny - New Zealand, Papua New Guinea
Anaphalioides subrigida (Colenso) Anderb - New Zealand North Island
Anaphalioides trinervis (G.Forst.) Anderb. - New Zealand
Anaphalioides.funFact: They are native to New Zealand and New Guinea.SpeciesAnaphalioides alpina (Cockayne) D.Glenny - New Zealand Anaphalioides bellidioides (G.Forst.) D.Glenny - New Zealand incl.
Anaphalioides.nativeRegion: They are native to New Zealand and New Guinea.SpeciesAnaphalioides alpina (Cockayne) D.Glenny - New Zealand Anaphalioides bellidioides (G.Forst.) D.Glenny - New Zealand incl.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Antón Vázquez, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Antón Vázquez
(c) James Gaither, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

Anguilla.description: Anguilla is a British Overseas Territory of the Caribbean region in the North Atlantic Ocean. It is one of the most northerly of the Leeward Islands in the Lesser Antilles, lying east of Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands and directly north of Saint Martin. The territory consists of the main island of Anguilla, approximately 16 miles long by 3 miles (5 km) wide at its widest point, together with a number of much smaller islands and cays with no permanent population. The territory's capital is The Valley. The total land area of the territory is 35 square miles (91 km2), with a population of approximately 15,753 in 2021.
Anguilla.funFact: It is one of the most northerly of the Leeward Islands in the Lesser Antilles, lying east of Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands and directly north of Saint Martin.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Сильченко Иван Владимирович, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Сильченко Иван Владимирович

Anomoea.description: Anomoea is a genus of leaf beetles in the subfamily Cryptocephalinae. The name is frequently confused with the tephritid fly genus Anomoia, due to historical confusion over precedence.
Anomoea.funFact: The name is frequently confused with the tephritid fly genus Anomoia, due to historical confusion over precedence.

Anoplocapros.description: Anoplocapros is a genus of marine ray-finned fishes belonging to the family Aracanidae, the deepwater boxfishes or temperate boxfishes. These fishes are endemic to the waters around Australia.
Anoplocapros.funFact: These fishes are endemic to the waters around Australia.
Anoplocapros.nativeRegion: These fishes are endemic to the waters around Australia.

Anoplolepis.description: Anoplolepis, also known as the "pugnacious ants", is a genus of ants in the subfamily Formicinae and tribe Lasiini. The genus is mainly found in the Afrotropics, with a few native species known from the Malagasy and Oriental regions.
Anoplolepis.funFact: The genus is mainly found in the Afrotropics, with a few native species known from the Malagasy and Oriental regions.
Anoplolepis.nativeRegion: The genus is mainly found in the Afrotropics, with a few native species known from the Malagasy and Oriental regions.

Anoplotrupes.description: Anoplotrupes is a genus of earth-boring dung beetles belonging to the family Geotrupidae subfamily Geotrupinae.

Anredera.description: Anredera is a genus of plants native to Latin America, the West Indies, Texas, and Florida. Some are naturalized in other regions. Most of them evergreen vines of dry scrubland and thickets. Members of the genus are commonly known as Madeira vines. At least one species, A. cordifolia, bears edible roots or tubers and leaves similar to those of Basella alba. The same species has become an invasive plant in many tropical and subtropical regions outside its natural range.
Anredera.funFact: Anredera is a genus of plants native to Latin America, the West Indies, Texas, and Florida.
Anredera.nativeRegion: Anredera is a genus of plants native to Latin America, the West Indies, Texas, and Florida.

Anseranas.description: The magpie goose is the sole living representative species of the family Anseranatidae. This waterbird is found in northern Australia and southern New Guinea. The species is not truly migratory but it sometimes travels large distances to find food and water, especially when not breeding, and is sometimes recorded outside its core range. The species was once also widespread in southern Australia but disappeared from there largely due to the drainage of the wetlands where the birds once bred. Due to their importance to Aboriginal people as a seasonal food source, as subjects of recreational hunting, and as a tourist attraction, their expansive and stable presence in northern Australia has been "ensured [by] protective management".
Anseranas.funFact: The species is not truly migratory but it sometimes travels large distances to find food and water, especially when not breeding, and is sometimes recorded outside its core range.
Anseranas.nativeRegion: This waterbird is found in northern Australia and southern New Guinea.

Antennaria.description: Antennaria is a genus of dioecious perennial herbs in the family Asteraceae, native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with a few species in temperate southern South America; the highest species diversity is in North America. Common names include catsfoot or cat's-foot, pussytoes and everlasting.
Antennaria.funFact: Antennaria is a genus of dioecious perennial herbs in the family Asteraceae, native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with a few species in temperate southern South America; the highest species diversity is in North America.
Antennaria.nativeRegion: Antennaria is a genus of dioecious perennial herbs in the family Asteraceae, native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, with a few species in temperate southern South America; the highest species diversity is in North America.

Anteos.description: Anteos is a genus of butterflies in the family Pieridae, commonly called angled-sulphurs.
Anteos.funFact: Anteos is a genus of butterflies in the family Pieridae, commonly called angled-sulphurs.

Antigone.description: In Greek mythology, Antigone was a Theban princess and a character in several ancient Greek tragedies. She was the daughter of Oedipus, king of Thebes; her mother/grandmother was either Jocasta or, in another variation of the myth, Euryganeia. She was the sister of Polynices, Eteocles, and Ismene.
Antigone.funFact: She was the daughter of Oedipus, king of Thebes; her mother/grandmother was either Jocasta or, in another variation of the myth, Euryganeia.

Apium.description: Apium is a genus, as currently circumscribed by Plants of the World Online, of 12 species of flowering plants in the family Apiaceae, with an unusual highly disjunct distribution with one species in the temperate Northern Hemisphere in the Western Palaearctic, and the rest in the temperate Southern Hemisphere in southern Africa, southern South America, Australia, and New Zealand. They are prostrate to medium-tall annual, biennial or perennial herbs growing up to 1 m high in wet soil, often marshes and salt marshes, and have pinnate to bipinnate leaves and small white flowers in compound umbels. Some species are edible, notably Apium graveolens, which is the wild ancestor of the commercially important vegetables celery, celeriac and leaf celery.
Apium.funFact: Some species are edible, notably Apium graveolens, which is the wild ancestor of the commercially important vegetables celery, celeriac and leaf celery.

Aplectrum.description: Aplectrum hyemale is a species of orchid native to the eastern United States and Canada, from Oklahoma east to the Carolinas and north to Minnesota, Ontario, Quebec and Massachusetts. It is particularly common in the Appalachian Mountains, the Great Lakes Region, and the Ohio and Upper Mississippi Valleys. Isolated populations are also reported from Arizona.
Aplectrum.funFact: Aplectrum hyemale is a species of orchid native to the eastern United States and Canada, from Oklahoma east to the Carolinas and north to Minnesota, Ontario, Quebec and Massachusetts.
Aplectrum.nativeRegion: Aplectrum hyemale is a species of orchid native to the eastern United States and Canada, from Oklahoma east to the Carolinas and north to Minnesota, Ontario, Quebec and Massachusetts.

Aplocera.description: Aplocera is a genus of moths of the family Geometridae.

Aplodinotus.description: The freshwater drum, Aplodinotus grunniens, is a fish endemic to North and Central America. It is the only species in the genus Aplodinotus, and is a member of the family Sciaenidae. It is the only North American member of the group that inhabits freshwater for its entire life. Its generic name, Aplodinotus, comes from Greek meaning "single back", and the specific epithet, grunniens, comes from a Latin word meaning "grunting". It is given to it because of the grunting noise that mature males make. This noise comes from a special set of muscles within the body cavity that vibrate against the swim bladder. The purpose of the grunting is unknown, but due to it being present in only mature males and during the spawning season, it is assumed to be linked to spawning.
Aplodinotus.funFact: The freshwater drum, Aplodinotus grunniens, is a fish endemic to North and Central America.
Aplodinotus.nativeRegion: The freshwater drum, Aplodinotus grunniens, is a fish endemic to North and Central America.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Quinn, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Quinn

Aplonis.description: Aplonis is a genus of starlings. These are essentially island species of Indonesia and Oceania, although some species' ranges extend to the Malay Peninsula, southern Vietnam and northeastern Queensland. The typical adult Aplonis starling is fairly uniformly plumaged in black, brown or dark green, sometimes with a metallic gloss. The eye ring is often distinctively coloured. Immatures of several species have dark streaked pale underparts.
Aplonis.funFact: These are essentially island species of Indonesia and Oceania, although some species' ranges extend to the Malay Peninsula, southern Vietnam and northeastern Queensland.

Apocynum.description: Apocynum, commonly known as dogbane or Indian hemp, is a small genus of the flowering plant family Apocynaceae. Its name comes from Ancient Greek ἀπόκυνον, from ἀπο- apo- "away" and κύων kyōn "dog", referring to dogbane, which was used to poison dogs. The genus is native to North America, temperate Asia, and southeastern Europe.
Apocynum.funFact: Apocynum, commonly known as dogbane or Indian hemp, is a small genus of the flowering plant family Apocynaceae.
Apocynum.nativeRegion: The genus is native to North America, temperate Asia, and southeastern Europe.

Apodasmia.description:
Apodasmia is a group of plants in the Restionaceae described as a genus in 1998. It is native to Australia, New Zealand, and Chile.SpeciesApodasmia brownii (Hook.f.) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - SA, Tas, Vic
Apodasmia chilensis (Gay) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - southern Chile
Apodasmia similis (Edgar) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - New Zealand
Apodasmia.funFact: It is native to Australia, New Zealand, and Chile.SpeciesApodasmia brownii (Hook.f.) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - SA, Tas, Vic Apodasmia chilensis (Gay) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - southern Chile Apodasmia similis (Edgar) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - New Zealand
Apodasmia.nativeRegion: It is native to Australia, New Zealand, and Chile.SpeciesApodasmia brownii (Hook.f.) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - SA, Tas, Vic Apodasmia chilensis (Gay) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - southern Chile Apodasmia similis (Edgar) B.G.Briggs & L.A.S.Johnson - New Zealand

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) bbi2, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by bbi2

Apodemia.description: Apodemia is a New World genus of metalmark butterflies found from Canada to Brazil.

Apogeshna.description: Apogeshna is a genus of moths of the family Crambidae.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Anita Gould, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Anita Gould
(c) magriet b, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA), uploaded by magriet b
(c) Andy Fyon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Andy Fyon
(c) Quinn, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Quinn
(c) Caiden B, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Caiden B
(c) Steve Garvie, some rights reserved (CC BY-SA)
(c) Juan Carlos Garcia Morales, all rights reserved, uploaded by Juan Carlos Garcia Morales

Argentina.description: Argentina, officially the Argentine Republic, is a country in the southern cone of South America. It covers an area of 2,780,085 km2 (1,073,397 mi2), making it the second-largest country in South America after Brazil, the fourth-largest country in the Americas, and the eighth-largest country in the world. Argentina shares the bulk of the Southern Cone with Chile to the west, and is also bordered by Bolivia and Paraguay to the north, Brazil to the northeast, Uruguay and the South Atlantic Ocean to the east, and the Drake Passage to the south. Argentina is a federal state subdivided into twenty-three provinces, and one autonomous city, which is the federal capital and largest city of the nation, Buenos Aires. The provinces and the capital have their own constitutions, but exist under a federal system. Argentina claims sovereignty over the Falkland Islands, South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands, the Southern Patagonian Ice Field, and a part of Antarctica.
Argentina.funFact: It covers an area of 2,780,085 km2 (1,073,397 mi2), making it the second-largest country in South America after Brazil, the fourth-largest country in the Americas, and the eighth-largest country in the world.

Argia.description: Argia is a genus of damselflies of the family Coenagrionidae and of the subfamily Argiinae. It is a diverse genus which contains about 114 species and many more to be described. It is also the largest genus in Argiinae. They are found in the Western Hemisphere. They are commonly known as dancers. Although the genus name comes from Ancient Greek: ἀργία, romanized: argia, lit. 'laziness', dancers are quite active and alert damselflies. The bluer Argia species may be confused with Enallagma species.
Argia.funFact: It is also the largest genus in Argiinae.
Argia.nativeRegion: They are found in the Western Hemisphere.

Argiope.description: The genus Argiope includes rather large orb weaver spiders that often have a strikingly coloured abdomen. These spiders are distributed throughout the world. Most countries in tropical or temperate climates host one or more Argiope species.
Argiope.funFact: Most countries in tropical or temperate climates host one or more Argiope species.
Argiope.nativeRegion: These spiders are distributed throughout the world.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) budak, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by budak

Argynnis.description: Argynnis is a genus of butterflies in the family Nymphalidae, one of several groups known as "fritillaries".

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Vladimir Bryukhov, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Vladimir Bryukhov

Argyria.description: Argyria or argyrosis is a condition caused by excessive exposure to chemical compounds of the element silver, or silver dust. The most dramatic symptom of argyria is that the skin turns blue or blue-gray, and is usually most prominent in sun-exposed areas of the skin. It may take the form of generalized argyria or local argyria. Generalized argyria affects large areas over much of the visible surface of the body. Local argyria shows in limited regions of the body, such as patches of skin, parts of the mucous membrane, or the conjunctiva.
Argyria.funFact: Argyria or argyrosis is a condition caused by excessive exposure to chemical compounds of the element silver, or silver dust.

Argyrotaenia.description: Argyrotaenia is a genus of moths in the tribe Archipini within the family Tortricidae.

Arhodia.description: Arhodia is a monotypic moth genus in the family Geometridae. Its only species, Arhodia lasiocamparia, the pink arhodia, is found in mainland Australia and Tasmania. Both the genus and species were first described by Achille Guenée in 1857.
Arhodia.funFact: Its only species, Arhodia lasiocamparia, the pink arhodia, is found in mainland Australia and Tasmania.
Arhodia.nativeRegion: Its only species, Arhodia lasiocamparia, the pink arhodia, is found in mainland Australia and Tasmania.

Ariadne.description: In Greek mythology, Ariadne was a Cretan princess, the daughter of King Minos of Crete. There are variations of Ariadne's myth, but she is known for helping Theseus escape from the Minotaur and being abandoned by him on the island of Naxos. There, Dionysus saw Ariadne sleeping, fell in love with her, and later married her. Many versions of the myth recount Dionysus throwing Ariadne's jeweled crown into the sky to create a constellation, the Corona Borealis.
Ariadne.funFact: There are variations of Ariadne's myth, but she is known for helping Theseus escape from the Minotaur and being abandoned by him on the island of Naxos.

Arigomphus.description:
Arigomphus is a genus of dragonflies of the Gomphidae family.
This group is commonly called the pond clubtails. The species are fairly plain and only the males have the club-shaped abdomen. Unlike other gomphids, they may emerge from artificial ponds.
Arigomphus.funFact: This group is commonly called the pond clubtails.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Kent Miller, some rights reserved (CC BY-ND), uploaded by Kent Miller

Arilus.description: Arilus, or wheel bugs due to the semicircular crest on the pronotum, is a genus of true bugs in the family Reduviidae, subfamily Harpactorinae and tribe Harpactorini. Most species are found in the Americas. Arilus is a generalist predator of insects.
Arilus.funFact: Arilus is a generalist predator of insects.
Arilus.nativeRegion: Most species are found in the Americas.

Aseroe.description: Aseroe is a small genus of basidiomycete fungi of the family Phallaceae, though sometimes placed in the separate family Clathraceae. The genus name is derived from the Ancient Greek words Asē/αση 'disgust' and roē/ροη 'juice'. The genus was described with the collection and description of the type species Aseroe rubra in 1800 by French botanist Jacques Labillardière. As with other stinkhorn-like fungi, mature fruiting bodies are covered with olive-brown slime, containing spores, which attracts flies. These fungi are common in mulch and are saprobic.
Aseroe.funFact: The genus name is derived from the Ancient Greek words Asē/αση 'disgust' and roē/ροη 'juice'.

Asimina.description: Asimina is a genus of small trees or shrubs described as a genus in 1763. Asimina is the only temperate genus in the tropical and subtropical flowering plant family Annonaceae. Asimina have large, simple leaves and large fruit. It is native to eastern North America and collectively referred to as pawpaw. The genus includes the widespread common pawpaw Asimina triloba, which bears the largest edible fruit indigenous to the United States. Pawpaws are native to 26 states of the U.S. and to Ontario in Canada. The common pawpaw is a patch-forming (clonal) understory tree found in well-drained, deep, fertile bottomland and hilly upland habitat. Pawpaws are in the same plant family (Annonaceae) as the custard apple, cherimoya, sweetsop, soursop, and ylang-ylang; the genus is the only member of that family not confined to the tropics. Fossils date to the Cretaceous.
Asimina.funFact: Asimina is the only temperate genus in the tropical and subtropical flowering plant family Annonaceae.
Asimina.nativeRegion: It is native to eastern North America and collectively referred to as pawpaw.

Asio.description: Asio is a genus of typical owls, or true owls, in the family Strigidae. This group has representatives over most of the planet, and the short-eared owl is one of the most widespread of all bird species, breeding in Europe, Asia, North and South America, the Caribbean, Hawaii and the Galápagos Islands. Its geographic range extends to all continents except Antarctica and Australia.
Asio.funFact: This group has representatives over most of the planet, and the short-eared owl is one of the most widespread of all bird species, breeding in Europe, Asia, North and South America, the Caribbean, Hawaii and the Galápagos Islands.

Asota.description: Asota is a town near Shewa Adda and Union Council of Swabi District in the Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa province of Pakistan. It is located at 34°14'0N 72°21'0E with an altitude of 340 metres.
Asota.funFact: It is located at 34°14'0N 72°21'0E with an altitude of 340 metres.

Asparagus.description: Asparagus is a genus of flowering plants in the family Asparagaceae, subfamily Asparagoideae. It comprises up to 300 species. Most are evergreen long-lived perennial plants growing from the understory as lianas, bushes or climbing plants. The best-known species is the edible Asparagus officinalis, commonly referred to as just asparagus. Some other members of the genus, such as Asparagus densiflorus, are grown as ornamental plants.
Asparagus.funFact: The best-known species is the edible Asparagus officinalis, commonly referred to as just asparagus.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) Derek de la Harpe, some rights reserved (CC BY-ND), uploaded by Derek de la Harpe

Asphodelus.description: Asphodelus is a genus of mainly perennial flowering plants in the asphodel family Asphodelaceae that was first described by Carl Linnaeus in 1753. The genus was formerly included in the lily family (Liliaceae). The genus is native to temperate Europe, the Mediterranean, Africa, the Middle East, and the Indian subcontinent, and some species have been introduced to, and are now naturalized in, other places such as New Zealand, Australia, Mexico and southwestern United States. Many asphodels are popular garden plants, which grow in well-drained soils with abundant natural light.
Asphodelus.funFact: Asphodelus is a genus of mainly perennial flowering plants in the asphodel family Asphodelaceae that was first described by Carl Linnaeus in 1753.
Asphodelus.nativeRegion: The genus is native to temperate Europe, the Mediterranean, Africa, the Middle East, and the Indian subcontinent, and some species have been introduced to, and are now naturalized in, other places such as New Zealand, Australia, Mexico and southwestern United States.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) David Gil, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by David Gil

Asphondylia.description: Asphondylia is a cosmopolitan genus of gall midges in the family Cecidomyiidae. All species in this genus induce galls on plants, especially on flowers and flower buds. There are over 300 described species in the genus Asphondylia, with many more likely to be discovered and described, especially in the southern hemisphere.
Asphondylia.funFact: There are over 300 described species in the genus Asphondylia, with many more likely to be discovered and described, especially in the southern hemisphere.

Aspidimorpha.description: Aspidimorpha is a large Old World genus of beetles belonging to the family Chrysomelidae and tribe Aspidimorphini. The genus name is frequently misspelled as "Aspidomorpha", due to an unjustified spelling change in 1848.
Aspidimorpha.funFact: The genus name is frequently misspelled as "Aspidomorpha", due to an unjustified spelling change in 1848.

Aspidoscelis.description: Aspidoscelis is a genus of whiptail lizards in the family Teiidae.

Aspidotis.description: Aspidotis is a small genus of leptosporangiate ferns known commonly as laceferns. Most species are native to slopes, ridges, and rocky outcroppings, primarily in California and Mexico, although one species included in the genus by some authorities is widely distributed in eastern Africa.
Aspidotis.funFact: Aspidotis is a small genus of leptosporangiate ferns known commonly as laceferns.
Aspidotis.nativeRegion: Most species are native to slopes, ridges, and rocky outcroppings, primarily in California and Mexico, although one species included in the genus by some authorities is widely distributed in eastern Africa.

defaultPhoto.attribution: (c) James Gaither, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC-ND)

Astraeus.description: In Greek mythology, Astraeus or Astraios is the son of Crius and Eurybia and the consort of Eos. He is said to be the father of the winds.
Astraeus.funFact: He is said to be the father of the winds.
